title: Roast leg of lamb
subtitle: Garlic and rosemary studded, carved pink
excerpt: Lamb leg studded with garlic and rosemary, roasted at moderate heat to pink throughout, rested, and carved. The defining British Easter roast. Served with mint sauce, roast potatoes, and spring vegetables. A 2 kg leg feeds six to eight people generously.
sourceNotes: Roast lamb with garlic and rosemary is the Easter roast of the British Isles. The combination predates written recipes and is found in French, Spanish, and Italian traditions as well. The British version is typically carved at the table, served pink, with mint sauce as the non-negotiable condiment.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Stud and season the night before, leave uncovered in the fridge.

content.text: Stud the lamb the day before if possible: the flavour of the garlic and rosemary migrates into the surrounding meat during the overnight rest. Use a small sharp knife to make deep incisions in the flesh, then push a sliver of garlic and a small sprig of rosemary into each hole.

Internal temperatures for lamb: 60°C for pink, 65°C for medium, 70°C for well done. Pink is the standard for a roast leg in the British tradition. Pull it out of the oven 5°C before your target and rest for at least 20 minutes.

Heat the oven to 200°C. Using a small sharp knife, make 16 deep incisions all over the {{lamb-leg}}. Push a sliver of {{garlic}} and a small piece of {{rosemary}} into each incision. Rub all over with {{olive-oil}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and {{black-pepper}}. Place in a roasting tin and roast for 25 minutes per 500 g for pink, or until a thermometer reads 60°C in the thickest part away from the bone. Transfer to a warm board, cover loosely with foil, and rest for 20 minutes. Carve by slicing parallel to the bone on both sides, then cutting the meat away from the bone in sections.

items.symptom: Garlic and rosemary burning and turning bitter
items.cause: Slivers pushed into the surface rather than deep into the flesh
items.fix: Make the incisions at least 3 cm deep. The aromatics must be inside the muscle, not on the surface, to avoid burning during the initial hot phase

items.symptom: Meat still pink at the bone when outer meat is perfect
items.fix: Normal. Rest longer and the carried heat will continue cooking the meat around the bone. If this bothers you, buy a boned and rolled leg instead

items.symptom: Crackling-style dry surface rather than moist
items.cause: Lamb dried out, not enough fat on the surface
items.fix: Use a higher olive oil quantity on the surface. A well-marbled leg should stay moist; leaner supermarket lamb may need more oil

content.text: Roast lamb is the Easter lunch in British, French, and Greek traditions simultaneously. In Britain it is paired with mint sauce, a combination that has been standard since at least the 18th century. The leg is the most common cut for a Sunday joint because it is easier to carve than a shoulder and cooks in a reasonable time. Spring lamb coincides with the Easter season, making this one of the few dishes where seasonality and occasion align.
